name: rune of mountains
shortDescription: A rune of mountains cut with heavy lines and patient geometry, traded by wardens, builders, and earth-minded mages who prefer power that refuses to move. It feels dense in the hand, as if every mark remembers stone under snow.
flavorText: “Some runes whisper. This one waits.”

name: fabled rune of destiny
shortDescription: A fabled rune of destiny, famous enough that even frauds copy its shape badly. Fortune tellers, nobles, and desperate commanders all want it for different reasons, though serious scholars warn that destiny is rarely improved by being purchased.
flavorText: “It does not tell the future. It charges you for believing in one.”

name: rune of Reshaping
shortDescription: A rune of Reshaping marked with shifting curves that never seem quite finished. Tailor-mages, sculptors, healers, and reckless experimenters value it as a focus, while careful merchants keep it away from wax seals and legal documents.
flavorText: “Change is useful until it learns your address.”

name: rough inert rune
shortDescription: A rough inert rune whose marks hold shape but no obvious spark, often bought for practice, study, or hopeful reawakening. Apprentices like its forgiving price, while masters inspect it for the faint possibility that sleep is not the same as emptiness.
flavorText: “A dead rune is cheaper. A sleeping one is only pretending.”

name: market activated rune
shortDescription: A market activated rune prepared for ordinary magical use, clear enough for vendors to demonstrate without frightening the whole street. It is not legendary, but reliable activation makes it valuable to practical buyers who prefer results over mysterious humming.
flavorText: “The best market magic works before the crowd gets bored.”

name: rune of abyss
shortDescription: A rune of abyss etched in dark strokes that seem deeper than the stone allows. It attracts occult scholars, sea priests, tunnel mages, and collectors of alarming things, all of whom ask whether the darkness is decorative while hoping it is not.
flavorText: “Look too long and the carving begins looking back by reputation alone.”

name: fabled rune of flames
shortDescription: A fabled rune of flames carrying old heat in its pattern, prized by fire-mages, forge cults, and nobles who enjoy owning dangerous warmth under glass. Even cold, it makes wax sweat and cautious merchants reach for sand.
flavorText: “Fire is easier to sell when trapped in grammar.”

name: rune of ice
shortDescription: A rune of ice with pale cuts and a chill that lingers after handling. It sells well in summer caravans, preservation rooms, and mage circles where cold is considered a tool rather than a complaint.
flavorText: “Winter pays no rent, but this rune keeps a room for it.”

name: rune of mystery
shortDescription: A rune of mystery whose marks resist easy naming, making it irresistible to collectors and deeply irritating to catalogers. It is useful in divination, concealment, and academic arguments, three markets that always pay for uncertainty.
flavorText: “If anyone explains it too confidently, raise the price.”

name: rune of life
shortDescription: A rune of life carved with warm, looping marks favored by healers, gardeners, midwives, and temple custodians. Buyers handle it gently, not because it is fragile, but because objects associated with life make people suddenly remember their manners.
flavorText: “The rune pulses softly, or perhaps everyone simply hopes it does.”

name: rune of action
shortDescription: A rune of action made for motion, impulse, and the useful ending of hesitation. Caravan captains, duel trainers, and impatient enchanters like it because its pattern suggests that standing still is merely a failure of imagination.
flavorText: “Some runes advise. This one shoves.”

name: rune of anger
shortDescription: A rune of anger cut with harsh strokes and a prickling edge to its presence. It is sold carefully to battle mages, ward designers, and ritualists who claim they need controlled fury, which is what everyone says before replacing a workbench.
flavorText: “Anger in stone is still anger.”

name: rune of light
shortDescription: A rune of light with clean lines and a faint brightness caught in the grooves. Miners, scribes, shrine keepers, and night traders all value it because good light saves candles, time, and occasionally fingers.
flavorText: “Darkness retreats faster when charged by the hour.”

name: rune of Long Dusk
shortDescription: A rune of Long Dusk shaded with twilight tones and old evening symbolism. It is sought by watchmen, dream scholars, and border mages who work in the uncertain hour when day has ended but night has not yet admitted responsibility.
flavorText: “Dusk is not a time. It is a negotiation.”

name: scarce rune of time
shortDescription: A scarce rune of time with tiny marks that make appraisers check their clocks twice. Scholars, aging nobles, and precision artificers pay well for even a questionable example, because everyone believes time owes them at least one favor.
flavorText: “It does not stop the hourglass. It makes the sand nervous.”

name: rune of discovery
shortDescription: A rune of discovery used by mapmakers, treasure seekers, librarians, and scholars who have misplaced patience. It does not guarantee answers, but it makes buyers feel that the next drawer, cave, shelf, or rumor may finally matter.
flavorText: “Discovery is mostly persistence with better lighting.”

name: market rune of the starcourt
shortDescription: A market rune of the starcourt, polished for public sale and marked with celestial court patterns fashionable among urban mages. It is less rare than court collectors pretend, but the name alone raises interest in any city with rooftops and ambition.
flavorText: “Stars are distant. Starcourt merchandise is very near the register.”

name: rune of rock
shortDescription: A rune of rock with blunt marks and a dependable weight, used in reinforcement charms, quarry rites, and stubborn little wards. It is not graceful magic, but buyers appreciate a rune that seems unlikely to change its mind.
flavorText: “Rock magic rarely flatters. It endures.”

name: scarce rune of the future
shortDescription: A scarce rune of the future that draws anxious buyers faster than almost any honest tool should. Seers inspect it with reverence, merchants insure it with suspicion, and nobles ask whether it can reveal profit before revealing consequences.
flavorText: “The future is always valuable until it arrives.”

name: rough rune of the past
shortDescription: A rough rune of the past, chipped and uneven but heavy with historical flavor. Archivists, ancestor cults, and memory-workers buy such pieces for study, while collectors simply enjoy owning something that appears to remember more than they do.
flavorText: “Old marks do not repeat themselves. They wait for fools to do it.”

name: scarce rune of waters
shortDescription: A scarce rune of waters marked with flowing cuts that seem smoother than the stone beneath them. River priests, ship mages, brewers, and irrigation guilds all find uses for it, which keeps the price moving like a tide.
flavorText: “Water obeys shape only until it finds a better one.”

name: rune of energy
shortDescription: A rune of energy with restless marks that catch the eye even when still. Artificers use it for motion, light, charge, and dramatic demonstrations, though careful merchants do not store too many together in the same drawer.
flavorText: “Potential is just trouble with good posture.”

name: rune of rocks
shortDescription: A rune of rocks, plural in both mark and mood, suited to scattered stone, gravel wards, construction charms, and stubborn terrain work. It is humbler than mountain magic but more flexible, which is often what builders actually need.
flavorText: “A mountain is impressive. A pile of rocks is useful.”

name: rune of slime
shortDescription: A rune of slime with glossy greenish marks and a reputation for adhesion, seepage, and unpleasantly clever alchemy. It sells to dungeon keepers, potion brewers, and experimental cooks who should probably not be encouraged.
flavorText: “Some magic sparkles. Some refuses to wash off.”

name: canvas sheet
shortDescription: A plain canvas sheet, strong enough for tents, covers, sails, packs, wall hangings, or emergency repairs when weather laughs at nicer fabric. It is honest trade cloth: bulky, durable, and never glamorous until rain begins falling.
flavorText: “Canvas is what plans wear when they expect mud.”

name: rune of decay
shortDescription: A rune of decay etched with tired lines and a dullness that seems to spread across nearby dust. Alchemists, grave scholars, and curse-breakers buy it for controlled study, a phrase that has comforted very few warehouse owners.
flavorText: “Decay is patient enough to make impatience look silly.”

name: rune of bones
shortDescription: A rune of bones carved with pale grooves and old anatomical symbols, used in funerary rites, skeletal wards, and unpleasantly practical magic. It is bought quietly, wrapped well, and rarely displayed beside the tea service.
flavorText: “Bone remembers the shape long after pride is gone.”

name: scarce rune of tombs
shortDescription: A scarce rune of tombs carrying the sealed mood of old doors and colder names. Grave wardens, historians, and necromantic specialists prize it, though sensible merchants mark the crate clearly so no clerk opens it after sunset.
flavorText: “Some locks are carved for the living to respect.”

name: rune of ice
shortDescription: A second rune of ice in a colder tradition, sharper and more ceremonial than common frost-work. Buyers compare it carefully with simpler ice runes, looking for purity, edge, and the quiet arrogance of winter preserved in stone.
flavorText: “Not all cold is the same. Some has lineage.”

name: scepter of force
shortDescription: A scepter of force balanced for command gestures, barrier rites, and the dramatic redirection of stubborn objects. It appeals to court mages and battlefield officers alike, because invisible pressure becomes more convincing when held in an expensive handle.
flavorText: “Politeness moves people slowly. Force is less patient.”

name: scepter of knowledge
shortDescription: A scepter of knowledge set with scholarly markings and a headpiece shaped for authority rather than comfort. Academies, judges, and ambitious tutors prize it as a symbol of learning, especially when actual wisdom is in shorter supply.
flavorText: “Knowledge carried like a weapon still expects to be obeyed.”

name: scepter of division
shortDescription: A scepter of division used in separation charms, boundary rituals, and disputes where one thing must become two. It is useful, unsettling stock, often purchased by mages who insist they only divide problems and never loyalties.
flavorText: “It cuts without an edge and argues without a voice.”

name: scepter of silence
shortDescription: A scepter of silence with a muted finish and a reputation for ending noise, testimony, music, and occasionally negotiation. Court officials and temple wardens value it highly, while bards keep a professional distance from the display case.
flavorText: “The quietest tools often sell with the loudest warnings.”

name: scepter of earth
shortDescription: A scepter of earth capped with stonework and grounded symbols, favored by builders, wardens, and mages who trust soil more than weather. It feels steady in the hand and sells best to buyers who appreciate magic with a foundation.
flavorText: “Earth magic does not hurry. It already knows where everything falls.”

name: scepter of confusion
shortDescription: A scepter of confusion with mismatched ornament and a head that seems slightly wrong from every angle. Trick-mages and interrogators buy it eagerly; merchants prefer selling it quickly before the inventory ledger starts looking uncertain.
flavorText: “Its first enchantment is making the price feel negotiable.”

name: fabled scepter of weight
shortDescription: A fabled scepter of weight, infamous for making burdens literal and authority heavy. It is valuable to gravity mages, collectors of rare implements, and nobles who enjoy symbolism a little too much.
flavorText: “Power is easier to respect when no one can lift it casually.”

name: scepter of moonlight
shortDescription: A scepter of moonlight polished with pale metal and night-bright stones, suited to dream rites, tide work, quiet ceremonies, and dramatic balcony appearances. It sells best after dusk, when customers are easier to flatter with silver glow.
flavorText: “Moonlight is sunlight that learned secrets.”

name: scepter of water
shortDescription: A scepter of water used in flow charms, vessel blessings, river rites, and weather arguments that require confidence. Sailors, brewers, healers, and canal officials all value it, proving that water magic has more customers than poetry admits.
flavorText: “Water answers the hand that understands where it wants to go.”

name: scepter of fire
shortDescription: A scepter of fire with warm metal, sharp red accents, and a market audience that includes forge mages, temple performers, and reckless heirs. It should be displayed away from curtains, oil, and anyone eager to demonstrate indoors.
flavorText: “Fire obeys until applause gets involved.”

name: scepter of wisdom
shortDescription: A scepter of wisdom carved with calm symbols and sold to leaders, sages, and patrons who enjoy owning what they hope to possess. Whether it grants insight is debated, but it certainly improves the posture of anyone holding it.
flavorText: “Wisdom is difficult to buy, but symbols are available.”

name: scepter of steel
shortDescription: A scepter of steel built with clean metalwork and a martial, disciplined feel. Battle mages, smith guilds, and stern officials prize it because steel promises order, edge, and the comforting possibility that magic can be inspected like a blade.
flavorText: “Steel is the language of certainty.”

name: fabled scepter of infinity
shortDescription: A fabled scepter of infinity wrapped in looping symbols that make appraisers pause before writing any final number. Collectors compete fiercely for it, partly for its magic and partly because endlessness is an excellent excuse for an endless price.
flavorText: “Infinity fits poorly in a ledger, but merchants keep trying.”

name: scepter of ice
shortDescription: A scepter of ice tipped with pale crystal and a chill that makes leather gloves feel like wisdom. It is favored by frost mages, preservation guilds, and nobles who want every room to understand their emotional range.
flavorText: “Cold authority leaves no fingerprints.”

name: scepter of worship
shortDescription: A scepter of worship made for temple rites, processions, vows, and the delicate business of making devotion visible. Priests inspect its symbols carefully, while merchants simply make sure it shines under candlelight.
flavorText: “Faith travels farther when carried on polished metal.”

name: scepter of fear
shortDescription: A scepter of fear with severe lines and ornament chosen to make silence arrive early. It is bought by wardens, warlocks, and collectors of intimidating tools, then stored where visitors can see it without being invited to touch.
flavorText: “Fear is rarely subtle, but it is punctual.”

name: scepter of frequency
shortDescription: A scepter of frequency tuned with rings, ridges, and resonant fittings that seem made for pulses rather than blows. Artificers use it in signal work, harmonic wards, and experiments that make glass cabinets hum with expensive unease.
flavorText: “Every object has a note. This one asks too many questions.”

name: scepter of freezing
shortDescription: A scepter of freezing more direct than ordinary ice craft, designed to halt motion, preserve matter, and make breath visible in rooms that were perfectly warm moments ago. It sells well where heat is common and caution is not.
flavorText: “Freezing is just a pause with sharp teeth.”

name: scepter of multiplication
shortDescription: A scepter of multiplication used in duplication theory, growth charms, and inventory fantasies too dangerous for apprentices. Merchants find it fascinating and alarming, because any tool that promises more goods also promises more accounting problems.
flavorText: “It doubles interest before it doubles anything useful.”

name: scarce scepter of precognition
shortDescription: A scarce scepter of precognition, sought by seers, gamblers, commanders, and merchants pretending not to be all three. Its value comes from the possibility of warning, though buyers rarely enjoy being warned about themselves.
flavorText: “Seeing ahead is profitable until the future notices.”

name: fabled glaive of Lantern-Summoning
shortDescription: A fabled glaive of Lantern-Summoning with a polearm silhouette and warm sigils along the blade. It is part weapon, part beacon, and part legend, coveted by night guards, relic hunters, and anyone who believes darkness should be answered dramatically.
flavorText: “It does not merely cut the dark. It calls witnesses.”
